_meta.description: Machine-readable banned phrases for re-engagement / reactivation outreach. Loadable by any domain sanitizer. Grouped by domain applicability and severity.

severity_levels.critical: Guaranteed to kill the re-engagement. Never use.
severity_levels.high: Strongly signals mass outreach or desperation. Avoid in almost all cases.
severity_levels.medium: Weakens the message. Acceptable only in specific documented contexts.
severity_levels.low: Cliché but not fatal. Replace when possible.

banned_phrases.rationale: Ironic framing that immediately signals mass outreach. Nobody says 'hey stranger' to someone they genuinely thought of.
banned_phrases.replacement_pattern: Start with a specific reference to them or a genuine thought. 'just saw [thing related to their profile]' or jump straight to content.

banned_phrases.rationale: Forces the recipient to acknowledge the gap. The gap is neutral — naming it makes it loaded. Triggers the 'why did I stop responding?' reflex.
banned_phrases.replacement_pattern: Skip the gap entirely. Reference something new or something real from the last conversation.

banned_phrases.rationale: Same as 'long time no talk' — gap-naming. Makes the receiver re-evaluate the silence.
banned_phrases.replacement_pattern: Jump over the gap. Start where you are now, not where you left off.

banned_phrases.rationale: Signals zero real reason to reach out. The phrase exists purely to fulfill an obligation, and the recipient knows it. Universally recognized as a cliché in every outreach context.
banned_phrases.replacement_pattern: Ask a real question, reference a real thing, or share something genuinely interesting. If you have nothing real to say, wait until you do.

banned_phrases.rationale: Pure sales-speak. Immediately tags the sender as operating from a script, not genuine interest.
banned_phrases.replacement_pattern: Name the actual topic. 're: the proposal from March' or 'I've been thinking about what you said about X.'

banned_phrases.rationale: Same as 'circling back' — corporate filler that signals the sender has nothing real to say.
Visible automation signal. Confirms to the recipient that this is the 4th follow-up in a sequence and they are on a list.
banned_phrases.replacement_pattern: If you need a follow-up, reframe it entirely. New subject line, new angle, new reason.

banned_phrases.rationale: Guilt-induction. Forces the recipient to either defend their silence or lie. Creates emotional pressure that makes non-response feel unkind, which triggers avoidance.
banned_phrases.replacement_pattern: Never address why they went quiet. Reference something real. If you need to address tension, do it directly and once: 'if i said something off, totally understand.'

banned_phrases.rationale: Presumptuous and clingy. Sets up a power dynamic that puts the sender in a position of seeking validation.
banned_phrases.replacement_pattern: Don't ask. Share something genuine. If she's going to miss you, make her realize it through what you're doing, not by asking.

banned_phrases.rationale: Self-deprecating in a way that doesn't land. If they might not remember you, give them context naturally. If they definitely remember you, the question is redundant.
banned_phrases.replacement_pattern: Provide natural context: 'we talked at [event] about [topic]' — or just reference the last real thing you discussed.

banned_phrases.phrase: i know it's been a while
banned_phrases.rationale: Gap-naming. Forces the receiver to sit with the gap before they can even read your message. Starting on the back foot.
banned_phrases.replacement_pattern: Jump straight to the content. The recipient knows how long it's been. You don't need to narrate it.

banned_phrases.rationale: Filler that precedes 100% of cold and form-letter outreach. The recipient pattern-matches it to 'this is a template' before they read the next line.
banned_phrases.replacement_pattern: Start with the real reason. Or skip formalities entirely: 'random thought' / 'quick one'.

banned_phrases.rationale: Same pattern as 'hope you're doing well.'
banned_phrases.replacement_pattern: Same: cut the filler, start with the substance.

banned_phrases.rationale: Draws attention to the gap and opens with an apology, which signals you've been thinking about this for a while. Undermines the casual tone.
banned_phrases.replacement_pattern: Skip the apology. Continue the conversation naturally. If you were genuinely in the wrong, address it directly once, not pre-emptively.

banned_phrases.phrase: i've been meaning to reach out
banned_phrases.rationale: Signals that you've been overthinking this for a while. The best re-engagements feel spontaneous, even when they're planned.
banned_phrases.replacement_pattern: Just reach out. Don't narrate the reaching out.

banned_phrases.rationale: Process narration. You don't need to tell them what you're doing — just do it.
banned_phrases.replacement_pattern: Skip the meta-commentary. Give them a reason to engage, not a label for what you're attempting.

banned_phrases.rationale: Passive-aggressive. Even if unintentional, the phrase is so associated with frustration that it immediately puts the recipient on defense.
banned_phrases.replacement_pattern: Restate the relevant point directly: 'to summarize the proposal: [X].' Leave out the passive reference to prior communication.

banned_phrases.phrase: following up on my last message
banned_phrases.rationale: Process narration that reminds them they already ignored you. Start with a fresh angle instead.
banned_phrases.replacement_pattern: New subject, new angle. Reference the same topic if needed, but frame it as new information: 'one more thought on [topic]...'
